1: ʾĂšer hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû hannāḇîʾ ʾel-Pĕlištîm bĕṭerem yakkeh parʿōh ʾet-ʿazzâ (bĕṭerem yakkeh parʿōh ʾet-ʿazzâ): the oracle heading dates the Philistine oracle "before Pharaoh attacked Gaza." This refers to Neco II's northward campaign in 609 BCE (when he also killed Josiah at Megiddo, 2 Kings 23:29) or a subsequent Egyptian incursion. The dating places the oracle before the event it partially describes — prophetic foreknowledge of Egyptian military action in Philistia. The Philistines occupied the SW coastal plain of Canaan (the five cities: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath).
2: Kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnēh mayim ʿōlîm mîṣṣāpôn wĕhāyû lĕnaḥal šôṭēp (hinnēh mayim ʿōlîm mîṣṣāpôn wĕhāyû lĕnaḥal šôṭēp): the flood imagery — waters rising from the north becoming an overflowing torrent (naḥal šôṭēp, lit. overflowing wadi) — appears also in Isaiah 8:7-8 for the Assyrian flood-invasion and in Jeremiah 46:7-8 for the Egyptian Nile-flood. Here it applies to Babylon ("from the north") overwhelming Philistia. Wĕšāṭĕpû ʾereṣ ûmĕlōʾāh ʿîr wĕyōšĕḇê ḇāh (Wĕšāṭĕpû ʾereṣ ûmĕlōʾāh): they will swamp the land and its fullness — wĕšāṭap (to overflow, flood) applied to military invasion creates the same metaphorical totality: nothing remains dry, nothing escapes the flood of judgment.
3: Miqqôl šaʿăṭat pārsôt ʾabbîrāyw mirrāʿaš lĕmerkĕḇōtāyw hāmôn galgillāyw (miqqôl šaʿăṭat pārsôt ʾabbîrāyw): the soundscape of approaching cavalry — the thunder of hoofs (šaʿăṭat pārsôt, the stamping/clattering of hooves), the rumbling of chariots (hāmôn galgillāyw, the roar of their wheels). The acoustic terror of Babylonian chariot-forces is described with intense attention to sound, matching the visual intensity of v2's flood image. Lōʾ-pānû ʾāḇôt ʾel-bānîm mĕrippayôn yādāyim (Lōʾ-pānû ʾāḇôt ʾel-bānîm mĕrippayôn yādāyim): "fathers will not turn back for their children — their hands go limp" — the rippayôn yādāyim (limpness/slackness of hands — Jeremiah 6:24; Isaiah 13:7; Ezekiel 7:17) is the physical manifestation of complete demoralization: soldiers so overcome with fear they cannot even reach for their children.
4: ʿAl-hayyôm haBBāʾ lĕšadōd ʾet-kol-Pĕlištîm lĕhaḵrît lĕṣōr ûlĕṣîdôn kol śārid ʿōzēr (lĕhaḵrît lĕṣōr ûlĕṣîdôn kol śārid ʿōzēr): Tyre and Sidon (Phoenicia) are included as Philistia's commercial and military allies — their remaining support (kol śārid ʿōzēr, every remaining helper) will be cut off. Kî-šōdēd YHWH ʾet-Pĕlištîm šĕʾērît ʾî Kaptôr (šĕʾērît ʾî Kaptôr): "for YHWH is destroying the Philistines, the remnant of the coastlands of Caphtor." Kaptôr (Crete or the Aegean region) is the Philistines' place of origin, cited also in Amos 9:7 ("Did I not bring up the Philistines from Caphtor?"). YHWH's sovereignty over the Philistines extends back to their own origin-migration.
5: Bāʾâ qorḥâ ʾel-ʿazzâ nidmĕtâ ʾašqĕlôn šĕʾērît ʿimqām (Bāʾâ qorḥâ ʾel-ʿazzâ nidmĕtâ ʾašqĕlôn): Gaza is shaved bald (qorḥâ, the mourning rite of head-shaving, Amos 8:10; Micah 1:16; Isaiah 15:2); Ashkelon is silenced/destroyed (nidmĕtâ, Niphal of dāmam). ʿAd-mātay titgōdĕdî (ʿAd-mātay titgōdĕdî): "how long will you slash yourself?" — gādad (to slash, cut) refers to the mourning practice of self-laceration forbidden to Israel (Leviticus 19:28; Deuteronomy 14:1) but practiced by neighboring peoples (cf. 1 Kings 18:28; Zechariah 13:6). The question implies the mourning is endless because the devastation is total.
6: Hôy ḥereḇ laYHWH ʿad-ʾānâ lōʾ tišqôṭî (Hôy ḥereḇ laYHWH ʿad-ʾānâ lōʾ tišqôṭî): the sword-lament — a unique personification in the OAN collection. The prophet addresses YHWH's sword: "O sword of YHWH, how long before you rest?" Hēʾāsĕpî ʾel-taʿrēḵ hĕrāgĕʿî wĕdōmî (Hēʾāsĕpî ʾel-taʿrēḵ hĕrāgĕʿî wĕdōmî): "Return to your sheath; be still and stop." The pathos of this plea — the prophet lamenting the extent of divine judgment — is comparable to the Confessions (Jeremiah 4:10; Jeremiah 14:13; Jeremiah 20:7). The sword is felt as a burden even when its action is just.
7: Êḵ tišqôṭî wĕYHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh ʾel-ʾašqĕlôn wĕʾel-ḥôp hayyām šāmmâ yĕʾādāh (wĕYHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh ʾel-ʾašqĕlôn): the rhetorical answer ends the oracle — "How can it be still when YHWH has given it its charge? Against Ashkelon and the seacoast — there he has set it." The sword cannot rest because it is executing a divinely-ordered commission (YHWH ṣiwwāh-lāh, YHWH commanded it). The lament of v6 is answered not with false comfort but with honest acknowledgment: the judgment is real, authorized, and specific. This frank dialogue with the divine sword's necessity reflects Jeremiah's mature prophetic voice — he does not deny the horror of judgment but locates it within YHWH's sovereign purpose.
